memes.label: 'Table of Contents'
memes.description: 'Table of Contents'

memes.label: Absentee Landlord
memes.description: The property owner who lived far from their tenement investments and delegated management to agents, identified by Riis as a principal obstacle to tenement reform.

memes.label: African-American Community
memes.description: The Black community of 1890s New York, concentrated in the Eighth Ward and Thompson Street areas, subject to systematic housing discrimination by landlords under what Jacob Riis called "the color line."

memes.label: Aid Society
memes.description: A generic term for the charitable relief organizations that provided emergency assistance to tenement families, which Riis viewed as necessary but insufficient responses to systemic poverty.

memes.label: Alderman Mullins
memes.description: A Tammany Hall alderman mentioned by Riis as an example of the political machinery that governed the tenement districts through patronage and corruption.

memes.label: Alfred T. White
memes.description: Brooklyn philanthropic builder and pioneer of the model tenement movement, whose Riverside Buildings Riis hailed as "the beau ideal of the model tenement for a great city like New York."

memes.label: Allen Street
memes.description: A Lower East Side street marking the boundary between the Jewish quarter and the Bowery.

memes.label: Almshouse
memes.description: The public institution for the destitute poor on Blackwell's Island, documented by Riis as the last stop for elderly and disabled tenement dwellers who had exhausted all other means of support — a grim terminal point in the tenement life cycle.

memes.label: Annexed District
memes.description: The territory north of Manhattan annexed to New York City in 1874, which Riis described as receiving the overflow of tenement population from the Lower East Side.

memes.label: AICP
memes.description: The Association for Improving the Condition of the Poor, one of New York's oldest and largest charitable organizations.

memes.label: Attorney Street
memes.description: Lower East Side

memes.label: Baby Farming
memes.description: The systematic neglect and starvation of infants for profit, a practice Riis called "an infinitely fiendish" form of child-murder that flourished in New York's tenement districts despite legislative attempts to regulate it.

memes.label: Bandit's Roost
memes.description: A notorious alley at 59½ Mulberry Street in the Bend district of Five Points, made famous by Jacob Riis's iconic 1888 photograph — the frontispiece of How the Other Half Lives

memes.label: Battle Row
memes.description: A notoriously violent tenement block in 1890s New York, named for the frequency of street fights and gang confrontations among its Irish residents.

memes.label: Baxter Street
memes.description: A street in Manhattan's immigrant quarter known for its dense rows of tenements and proximity to Bottle Alley, documented by Jacob Riis.

memes.label: Bayard Street
memes.description: A street in the Lower East Side that Riis identified as the boundary between the Jewish and Italian immigrant colonies — a geographic fault line where the ethnic geography of the tenement districts was most clearly visible.

memes.label: Black-and-Tan
memes.description: Racially integrated saloons and dance halls in 1890s New York where Black and white patrons mixed, documented by Riis as sites of "common debauch" that challenged the city's racial boundaries.

memes.label: Blackwell's Island
memes.description: An island in the East River that housed the city's major penal and welfare institutions — the penitentiary, the almshouse, the workhouse, and the lunatic asylum — where the "Nether Half" was hidden away from the city's sight.

memes.label: Bleecker Street
memes.description: A street running through Greenwich Village and the Lower Manhattan borderlands — home to a French immigrant enclave and, by the 1890s, a contested boundary zone where Italian immigrants pressed northward into the French quarter, pushing African American settlement uptown in a three-way ethnic succession that Riis mapped in color.

memes.label: Blind Man's Alley
memes.description: A notorious rear alley tenement in lower Manhattan where blind beggars lived in squalid conditions, owned by the blind landlord Daniel Murphy.

memes.label: Board of Health
memes.description: The municipal public health authority of New York City, established in 1866 in response to a cholera epidemic, which led early efforts to regulate tenement housing conditions through its sanitary inspection powers.

memes.label: Bohemian Immigrants
memes.description: Czech immigrants from Bohemia who formed a distinctive enclave on Manhattan's East Side in the 1890s, known for tenement-house cigarmaking and their proud, isolated community.

memes.label: Bohemian Quarter
memes.description: Manhattan district settled by Bohemian (Czech) immigrants, known for tenement-house cigarmaking.

memes.label: Bootblacks
memes.description: The shoe-shining children of the tenement streets, below newsboys in Riis's hierarchy of street labor.

memes.label: Bottle Alley
memes.description: A notorious tenement alley off Baxter Street in Manhattan's Fourth Ward, documented by Jacob Riis as a representative example of slum housing conditions.

memes.label: Bowery
memes.description: The main commercial thoroughfare of the Lower East Side, lined with cheap lodging-houses, saloons, and pawnshops — the borderland between the tenement districts and the respectable city in Riis's urban geography

memes.label: Bread and Blood Riots
memes.description: A series of violent labor demonstrations and clashes in Tompkins Square during the 1870s that Riis cited as evidence for the pacifying effect of urban parks — ended, in his account, by the transformation of the square from a barren sand lot into a landscaped public park.

memes.label: Broad Street
memes.description: Financial District

memes.label: Brooklyn Bridge
memes.description: The iconic suspension bridge connecting Manhattan and Brooklyn, completed in 1883. Riis mentioned it as a landmark of the tenement geography, where the housing conditions on the Manhattan side stood in sharp contrast with Brooklyn's model tenements.

memes.label: Broome Street
memes.description: A major Lower East Side thoroughfare lined with garment-worker tenements.

memes.label: Canal Street
memes.description: A major thoroughfare forming the north boundary of Chinatown.

memes.label: Caroline Astor
memes.description: Prominent New York socialite and philanthropist (1830–1908) whose charitable work on behalf of tenement children Riis cites as an example of how the wealthy could productively engage with the poverty crisis

memes.label: Catherine Street
memes.description: Lower East Side

memes.label: Cellar Dwelling
memes.description: The lowest and most degraded form of tenement housing in 1890s New York — subterranean rooms below street level, lacking light, air, and drainage, inhabited by the city's most desperate poor.

memes.label: Charity
memes.description: The network of private philanthropic organizations in 1890s New York that Riis viewed as both a necessary relief mechanism and a deeply inadequate response to structural poverty

memes.label: Charity Organization Society
memes.description: The central coordinating body for private charity in 1890s New York, whose statistical investigations of poverty provided Riis with the quantitative foundation for his reform arguments

memes.label: Charles Loring Brace
memes.description: Founder of the Children's Aid Society, architect of the 'orphan train' program.

memes.label: Charles Scribner's Sons
memes.description: The New York publishing house that first published Jacob Riis's *How the Other Half Lives* in 1890, bringing the exposé of tenement conditions to a national audience.

memes.label: Chatham Square
memes.description: A major transit hub and public square in Lower Manhattan's Fourth Ward, offering a vantage point over the immigrant districts documented by Jacob Riis.

memes.label: Chatham Street
memes.description: The southern extension of the Bowery, lined with cheap lodging-houses and pawnshops, serving as the entry point for newly arrived immigrants and young men seeking work in New York

memes.label: Cherry Hill
memes.description: A once-fashionable neighborhood in the Fourth Ward of Lower Manhattan, later deteriorated into a notorious slum district documented by Jacob Riis.

memes.label: Cherry Street
memes.description: A Lower East Side street running along the East River waterfront — once the site of George Washington's presidential residence, later the epicenter of the earliest tenement construction, including the original rear houses

memes.label: Child Labor
memes.description: The employment of children in tenement industries, street trades, and factories — documented by Riis as both an economic necessity for poor families and a moral scandal that perpetuated the cycle of poverty

memes.label: Children's Aid Society
memes.description: Founded in 1853 by Charles Loring Brace, the primary charitable organization serving homeless and working children in New York's tenement districts — operating lodging-houses, industrial schools, and the orphan trains that relocated city children to rural families

memes.label: Chinese Immigrants
memes.description: A small but intensely scrutinized immigrant community in 1890s New York, concentrated in Chinatown on Mott Street, characterized by Jacob Riis as clean, quiet, and commercially shrewd but morally dangerous through opium and interracial relationships.

memes.label: Cholera Epidemic of 1866
memes.description: The epidemic that killed thousands and led to creation of the Metropolitan Board of Health.

memes.label: Chrystie Street
memes.description: A Lower East Side street at the western edge of the tenement quarter.

memes.label: City Hall Park
memes.description: The park surrounding New York's City Hall, a civic space whose proximity to the tenement districts was a visual irony Riis noted.

memes.label: City Mission Society
memes.description: A Protestant missionary organization active in New York's tenement districts, providing religious outreach and social services to the immigrant poor.

memes.label: Clinton Street
memes.description: A street marking the eastern edge of the Jewish Lower East Side.

memes.label: College Settlement
memes.description: A settlement house established by educated women who lived among the poor to provide education, recreation, and social services — cited by Riis as an example of promising tenement reform work.

memes.label: Immigrant Colony
memes.description: The ethnic enclaves that structured immigrant settlement in Gilded Age New York — geographically concentrated communities defined by shared language, origin, and institutions, which Riis mapped as distinct "colonies" each with their own character, economy, and place in the tenement hierarchy

memes.label: Columbia Street
memes.description: Lower East Side

memes.label: Concepts
memes.description: Index of all concept pages in How the Other Half Lives.

memes.label: Contagious Disease
memes.description: The epidemics that swept the tenements — cholera, typhus, smallpox — which Riis used to argue reform was self-protection for all classes.

memes.label: Corlears Hook
memes.description: A tenement district on a bend of the East River, known for poverty and waterfront gangs.

memes.label: Council of Hygiene
memes.description: A citizens' reform organization of physicians and civic leaders that conducted the first systematic sanitary survey of New York tenement districts in the 1860s, providing the factual groundwork for the creation of the Board of Health and the Tenement-House Act of 1867.

memes.label: Crime
memes.description: Riis portrayed crime in the tenement districts as an environmental product — bred by overcrowding, poverty, and the failure of housing — rather than an individual moral failing, with particular attention to juvenile delinquency and the "nurseries of crime" in the lodging-houses and back-alleys

memes.label: Crime & Vice
memes.description: Thematic index of pages documenting crime, vice, gangs, and policing in the tenement districts of Riis's New York.

memes.label: Crosby Street
memes.description: A street in the Lower East Side tenement district, part of the neighborhood that Riis documented as a zone of intense overcrowding and immigrant poverty.

memes.label: Daniel Murphy
memes.description: The blind landlord who owned Blind Man's Alley in the Fourth Ward, documented by Riis as a symbol of the tenement system's moral indifference — a property owner who profited from the worst housing while unable to see its conditions.

memes.label: Darkness in Tenements
memes.description: The absence of natural light in tenement rooms, which Riis identified as a cause of both physical disease and moral degradation.

memes.label: Delancey Street
memes.description: A major east-west thoroughfare in the Lower East Side, the commercial spine of the tenement district.

memes.label: Disease
memes.description: The epidemic and endemic illnesses — cholera, typhus, tuberculosis, measles — that ravaged tenement districts and provided the sanitary reform movement with its most urgent evidence for intervention

memes.label: Dive / Cellar Dive
memes.description: The underground cellar establishments of the tenement districts — unlicensed drinking dens where the poorest gathered, documented by Riis as the most degraded spaces in New York's slum ecology.

memes.label: Division Street
memes.description: A Lower East Side street marking the boundary between Chinatown and the Jewish quarter.

memes.label: Double Alley
memes.description: A rear tenement alley in the Lower East Side, representative of the double-row tenement configuration that Riis condemned as the worst form of tenement construction.

memes.label: Doyers Street
memes.description: A short, curved street in Manhattan's Chinatown known as "the Bloody Angle" for its history of tong-related violence, located in the tenement district Riis described in Chapter IX.

memes.label: Dumbbell Tenement
memes.description: The tenement design mandated by the 1879 Tenement House Act, with narrow air shafts creating a dumbbell-shaped floor plan.

memes.label: East River
memes.description: The tidal strait separating Manhattan from Brooklyn and Queens, which served as both a geographical boundary and a symbolic dividing line between the tenement districts of Manhattan and the model tenement experiments of Brooklyn in How the Other Half Lives.

memes.label: Eighth Ward
memes.description: A Manhattan ward encompassing the Thompson Street area, significant in Riis's account as the center of Black settlement and the "color line" in tenement housing.

memes.label: Elbridge T. Gerry
memes.description: New York lawyer and reformer who co-founded and served as President of the Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Children (SPCC), pioneering the legal protection of abused and exploited children in late 19th-century New York

relations.label: Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Children

memes.label: Eldridge Street
memes.description: A major street in the Jewish quarter of the Lower East Side, home to the Eldridge Street Synagogue and a center of Jewish immigrant life in the 1890s.

memes.label: Elizabeth Street
memes.description: A street on Manhattan's Lower East Side known for its tenement sweatshops, police station, and immigrant working-class life documented by Jacob Riis.

memes.label: Ellen Collins
memes.description: A nineteenth-century New York housing reformer whose pioneering model tenement experiment on Water Street proved to Jacob Riis and his readers that improving slum property could be a profitable business investment, not an act of charity.

memes.label: Ernest Flagg
memes.description: Architect and tenement reformer who designed model tenements with improved light and ventilation.

memes.label: Essex Street
memes.description: A principal artery of the Jewish Lower East Side, lined with tenements and pushcart markets.

memes.label: Ethnic Groups
memes.description: Index of all ethnic-group pages in How the Other Half Lives.

memes.label: Fan Tan
memes.description: A Chinese gambling game popular in the Chinatown tenements of 1890s New York, which Riis documented as part of the "vices" of the Chinese quarter.

memes.label: Featured Content
memes.description: Curated index of featured and premium-quality pages in How the Other Half Lives — the wiki's highest-quality content on tenement life, reform, and immigrant New York.

memes.label: Felix Adler
memes.description: Founder of the Ethical Culture movement, established the first free kindergarten in the tenement districts.

memes.label: Fifth Avenue
memes.description: Manhattan's premier residential avenue, which Riis contrasted with the tenement districts to dramatize the city's inequality — the "other half" of his title.

memes.label: Fire
memes.description: The constant threat of fire in overcrowded, wooden tenements — a recurring disaster that Riis uses to illustrate both the physical danger of slum housing and the failure of building regulations

memes.label: First Avenue
memes.description: A major north-south avenue on the East Side of Manhattan, running parallel to the East River, which Riis documented as the site of severe tenement overcrowding and the territory of the Rag Gang.

memes.label: Five Points
memes.description: The original New York slum district of the early 19th century, centered on the intersection of five streets in the Sixth Ward — a predecessor to the Bend that established the pattern of tenement overcrowding Riis documented

memes.label: Five Points House of Industry
memes.description: A charitable institution in the Five Points slum district that operated a nursery and industrial school serving over four hundred day scholars — cited by Riis as one of the organizations working to rescue children from the tenement environment.

memes.label: Five Points Mission
memes.description: A pioneering Christian rescue mission established in the former Old Brewery building in the Five Points district, one of the earliest organized efforts to aid the slum's destitute children — known alongside the Five Points House of Industry as one of "two powerful agents that were among the pioneers in this work of moral and physical regeneration."

memes.label: Forsyth Street
memes.description: A Lower East Side street parallel to the Bowery.

memes.label: Foundling Asylum
memes.description: A Catholic charitable institution in New York City that cared for abandoned infants, which Riis cited as one of the few barriers between tenement-born children and utter destruction.

memes.label: Fourth Ward
memes.description: One of the original slum districts of Lower Manhattan and the "cradle of the tenement" in 1890s New York, where the city's housing crisis first took root.

memes.label: Franklin Square
memes.description: Civic Center

memes.label: Fresh Air Fund
memes.description: A charitable organization that provided tenement children with summer excursions to the countryside — cited by Riis as an example of the practical charity that gave slum children their only experience of fresh air and green space.

memes.label: Front Tenement
memes.description: The street-facing tenement building — better than the rear tenement but still inadequate.

memes.label: German Immigrants
memes.description: The second-largest immigrant group in 1890s New York, established earlier than most tenement populations and noted by Jacob Riis for their cleanliness, industriousness, and love of flowers even in poverty.

memes.label: Goerck Street
memes.description: A Lower East Side tenement street near the East River.

memes.label: Gotham Court
memes.description: A notorious tenement block on Cherry Street, originally built as a model tenement but rapidly degraded into one of the worst slums in the city — Riis's prime example of reform failure

memes.label: Gouverneur Slip
memes.description: Lower East Side

memes.label: Gouverneur Street
memes.description: Lower East Side

memes.label: Grand Street
memes.description: The primary commercial thoroughfare of the Jewish Lower East Side.

memes.label: Growler / Growler Gang
memes.description: The tin can used to fetch beer from saloons — and the youth gangs that coalesced around the practice — documented by Riis as an early entry point into tenement crime for immigrant children.

memes.label: Hamilton Street
memes.description: A street on Manhattan's Lower East Side that once extended to the waterfront — according to a local tradition recorded by Jacob Riis, "the river came clear up here to Hamilton Street," and the street was home to "The Ship," a ramshackle tenement famous for fifty years that embodied the eccentric character of the pre-reform tenement landscape.

memes.label: Board of Health
memes.description: The municipal agency responsible for enforcing sanitary regulations in New York's tenements, which Riis both praised for its efforts and criticized for its inadequate powers.

memes.label: Health Department
memes.description: The New York City municipal agency responsible for tenement inspection, sanitary enforcement, and disease control in the 1890s, granted broad powers after the cholera epidemics.

memes.label: Hebrew Quarter
memes.description: The densely populated Jewish immigrant quarter of Manhattan's Lower East Side, centered on Baxter Street.

memes.label: Hell's Kitchen
memes.description: A notorious West Side tenement slum district in 1890s Manhattan, home to the Hell's Kitchen Gang and a heavy Irish immigrant population, known for its three-cent whiskey saloons, violent gang warfare, and pervasive political corruption.

memes.label: Henry Street
memes.description: Lower East Side

memes.label: Henry Street Settlement
memes.description: The settlement house founded by Lillian Wald on the Lower East Side, providing nursing and social services.

memes.label: Hester Street
memes.description: A street in the heart of the Lower East Side's Jewish quarter, where Riis documented the tenement sweatshops and pushcart markets of "Jewtown."

memes.label: Homelessness
memes.description: The crisis of chronic homelessness in late 19th-century New York City, where tens of thousands of men, women, and children lacked permanent shelter and relied on lodging-houses, police stations, and the streets.

memes.label: Houston Street
memes.description: A major east-west thoroughfare in lower Manhattan that Riis identified as a boundary of tenement misery — the street where cellar clearances, evictions, and the failures of housing reform were most visible.

memes.label: Immigrant Poverty
memes.description: The economic condition of newly arrived immigrants, concentrated in tenement districts with limited employment options.

memes.label: Immigration
memes.description: The massive influx of European and Chinese migrants into New York City in the 19th century that drove tenement demand and reshaped the city's ethnic geography, documented by Riis as both cause and consequence of the housing crisis

memes.label: Immigration & Settlement
memes.description: Thematic index of pages covering immigration, ethnic communities, and settlement patterns in the tenement districts — the human geography of Riis's "other half."

memes.label: Improved Dwellings Association
memes.description: A philanthropic stock corporation founded in the early 1880s to construct and operate model tenement housing in New York City, cited by Jacob Riis as one of the two foremost experiments in model tenement building.

memes.label: Inspector Byrnes
memes.description: Chief of the New York Police Department Detective Bureau in the 1880s–1890s, whose testimony about crime breeding in cheap lodging-houses provided Riis with key evidence for his reform arguments

memes.label: Irish Immigrants
memes.description: The largest and most politically influential immigrant group in 1890s New York, concentrated on the West Side tenement districts and wielding significant power through Tammany Hall and the saloon.

memes.label: Italian Immigrants
memes.description: The largest single immigrant group in 1890s New York tenements, concentrated in the Mulberry Bend area and known for dominating the ragpicking trade under the exploitative padrone system.

memes.label: Jacob Riis
memes.description: Danish-American social reformer, journalist, and photographer (1849–1914), author of How the Other Half Lives (1890), whose exposé of New York tenement conditions helped launch the Progressive-era housing reform movement

memes.label: James Street
memes.description: Lower East Side

memes.label: Jefferson Street
memes.description: Lower East Side

memes.label: Jewish Immigrants
memes.description: A densely crowded immigrant community of Eastern European Jews in 1890s New York, concentrated in the Tenth Ward ("Jewtown") and defined by the garment sweatshop economy, extreme thrift, and a religious life adapted to tenement conditions.

memes.label: Jewtown
memes.description: The Jewish quarter of the Tenth Ward, the most densely populated district in New York City — where Riis documented the extraordinary concentration of tenement life, the garment sweating system, and the complex internal economy of the largest Jewish community in America

memes.label: Josephine Shaw Lowell
memes.description: Charity reformer and founder of the Charity Organization Society.

memes.label: Joss House
memes.description: The Chinese temple and clubhouse on Mott Street in Chinatown, documented by Riis as a center of Chinese religious and social life — a "pagan" institution that he portrayed with a mixture of anthropological curiosity and nativist condescension.

memes.label: Juvenile Delinquency
memes.description: The phenomenon of child crime in the tenement districts, which Riis treated as a direct consequence of housing conditions that left children without supervision, education, or moral guidance.

memes.label: King's Daughters
memes.description: A Christian charitable organization of women that Riis cited in connection with the Neighborhood Guilds and tenement reform work, active in New York's poorest districts.
aliases: International Order of the King's Daughters

memes.label: Labor
memes.description: The system of immigrant labor in 1890s New York tenements, encompassing sweatshop garment work, cigarmaking, ragpicking, and day labor — the economic foundation of tenement life as documented by Riis.

memes.label: The Landlord
memes.description: The tenement property owner in Riis's narrative — both the specific, often immigrant small-holder who lived among his tenants and the absentee speculator who never set foot in his buildings — portrayed as the central antagonist of the housing reform story

memes.label: Lawrence Veiller
memes.description: Tenement reformer and key architect of the 1901 Tenement House Act.

memes.label: Lewis Street
memes.description: Lower East Side

memes.label: Lillian Wald
memes.description: Founder of the Henry Street Settlement, public health nurse, and tenement reformer.

memes.label: Lodger
memes.description: An individual renting space within a tenement apartment — typically a single room or even a share of a room — whose presence both supplemented the primary tenant's income and intensified overcrowding

memes.label: Lodger Evil
memes.description: The practice of tenement families taking in boarders, which Riis identified as a driver of overcrowding and disease.

memes.label: Lodging House
memes.description: Extremely cheap overnight accommodation in the Bowery and lower Manhattan that Riis identified as a breeding ground for crime and vagrancy among young men new to the city

memes.label: Lower East Side
memes.description: Densely populated immigrant neighborhood on Manhattan's east side, the primary setting for Riis's investigations.

memes.label: Ludlow Street
memes.description: A densely built street in the heart of New York's Tenth Ward "Jewtown," featuring seven-story tenements packed with immigrant families and serving as a center of the garment sweatshop economy documented by Jacob Riis.

memes.label: Madison Street
memes.description: A street on Manhattan's Lower East Side that contained one of the tenement alleys whose deadly fire "came home to New Yorkers one morning" — a cramped building with inaccessible fire escapes where, in the 1880s, women and children were "burned up… to the number of a full half score," an event that Riis used to indict the alley tenement as a building typology designed for catastrophe.

memes.label: Mangin Street
memes.description: Lower East Side

memes.label: Market Street
memes.description: Lower East Side

memes.label: Martha Lamb
memes.description: American historian (1826–1893) whose History of the City of New York (1877) documented the early tenement era and whose observations Riis cites as authoritative confirmation of his own reporting

memes.label: Model Tenement
memes.description: Philanthropic and reform-driven attempts to construct improved tenement housing with adequate light, ventilation, and sanitation, championed by Riis as the path out of the slum crisis

memes.label: Monroe Street
memes.description: A Lower East Side street near the East River.

memes.label: Morrisania
memes.description: A neighborhood in the annexed district of the Bronx, which Riis mentioned as receiving African-American families displaced from the downtown tenements.

memes.label: Most Referenced Pages
memes.description: Statistical ranking of the most-referenced pages in How the Other Half Lives wiki, sorted by total cross-reference count — a measure of each topic's centrality to Riis's narrative.

memes.label: Mott Street
memes.description: The main thoroughfare of Manhattan's Chinatown in the 1890s — a street of tenements, opium dens, and Chinese businesses that Riis described as the most insular and least understood of the city's immigrant enclaves

memes.label: Mulberry Bend
memes.description: Notorious tenement district at the curve of Mulberry Street, synonymous with urban squalor in 1880s New York.

memes.label: Mulberry Street
memes.description: The central artery of the Italian immigrant district in 1890s New York, running through the heart of the Five Points area and containing the notorious Mulberry Bend — described by Jacob Riis as one of the city's "worst plague-spots."

memes.label: Murray Hill
memes.description: A middle-class Manhattan neighborhood that marked one of the boundaries between tenement districts and more prosperous residential areas in 1890s New York.

memes.label: Neighborhood Guild
memes.description: Community-based settlement organizations that Riis cited alongside the College Settlement as examples of effective grassroots reform in the tenement districts.

memes.label: Newsboys
memes.description: The street children who sold newspapers — the 'aristocrats' of Riis's street Arabs, working but homeless.

memes.label: Newsboys' Lodging House
memes.description: A charitable lodging house at Duane Street established by the Children's Aid Society in 1855, providing shelter, education, and moral guidance for homeless newsboys and bootblacks — a flagship institution of the city's child-saving movement that Riis held up as a rare example of charity that actually worked.

memes.label: Norfolk Street
memes.description: A Lower East Side street in the Jewish quarter.

memes.label: Oak Street
memes.description: A street in the Lower East Side tenement district, representative of the narrow, densely built blocks that characterized the old-law tenement neighborhoods.

memes.label: Old London
memes.description: A term Riis used to refer to the London tenement districts that served as a point of comparison — and occasional contrast — for New York's tenement conditions.

memes.label: Oliver Street
memes.description: Lower East Side

memes.label: Opium
memes.description: The narcotic whose dens in Chinatown — concentrated on Mott and Pell Streets — represented for Riis the moral and physical degradation that tenement poverty enabled, though his account drew on racialized stereotypes about Chinese immigrants

memes.label: Organizations
memes.description: Index of all organization pages in How the Other Half Lives.

memes.label: Orchard Street
memes.description: A Lower East Side street known for pushcart markets, in the heart of the Jewish quarter.

memes.label: Overcrowding
memes.description: The defining physical condition of the tenement districts — extreme population density that compressed multiple families into single apartments, creating the sanitary crisis, moral dangers, and human degradation that Riis made the central subject of his exposé

memes.label: Padrone System
memes.description: The labor brokerage system by which Italian immigrants were recruited and controlled — the padrone (or "banker") acted as intermediary between employers and immigrant workers, extracting fees and commissions that Riis described as a form of debt bondage unique to the Italian immigrant experience

memes.label: Panic of 1893
memes.description: The severe economic depression that exacerbated tenement poverty and unemployment in the years after Riis's book.

memes.label: Park Street
memes.description: A street in the Five Points district, part of the notorious slum neighborhood.

memes.label: Pauperism
memes.description: The condition of chronic, multi-generational dependency on charity — distinguished by Riis and the Charity Organization Society from temporary poverty caused by unemployment or illness

memes.label: Pell Street
memes.description: A street in the heart of 1890s Chinatown, notorious for its opium dens, vice houses, and seven-cent lodging-houses documented by Jacob Riis in *How the Other Half Lives*.

memes.label: People
memes.description: Index of all person pages in How the Other Half Lives.

memes.label: Pike Street
memes.description: A Lower East Side street near the East River waterfront.

memes.label: Places
memes.description: Index of all place pages in How the Other Half Lives.

memes.label: Police Headquarters
memes.description: The central police headquarters at 300 Mulberry Street, which Riis documented as a gathering point for the city's lost and homeless children and a key institution in his campaign for tenement reform.

memes.label: Policy Game
memes.description: An illegal lottery popular in African-American tenement communities in 1890s New York, which Riis condemned as exploiting the poor while acknowledging its role as a desperate economic escape attempt.

memes.label: Polish Jew
memes.description: Polish Jewish immigrant community on Manhattan's Lower East Side, described extensively in Riis's study.

memes.label: Potter's Field
memes.description: The municipal burial ground for New York's indigent dead on Hart Island, where one in ten who died in the city was interred — for Riis, the statistical endpoint of the tenement system's human cost

memes.label: Prince Street
memes.description: A Lower East Side street north of Houston Street.

memes.label: Prison Association of New York
memes.description: A 19th-century criminal justice reform organization that documented conditions in New York prisons, investigated the environmental causes of crime, and whose Secretary testified before a landmark legislative committee linking tenement conditions to criminal behavior.

relations.label: Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Children

memes.label: Rear Tenement
memes.description: The original form of the New York tenement — a building erected behind an existing street-front house, typically on the rear of the lot, with no direct access to street light or air

memes.label: Reform Topics
memes.description: Thematic index of pages covering tenement reform, housing improvement, sanitary regulation, and public health in Riis's How the Other Half Lives.

memes.label: Richard Watson Gilder
memes.description: Editor of The Century Magazine and member of the Tenement House Committee.

memes.label: Ridge Street
memes.description: A Lower East Side street with densely packed tenements.

memes.label: Rivington Street
memes.description: A street on the Lower East Side of Manhattan that marked the southern boundary of the Jewish tenement district in the 1890s — the zone between Rivington and Division Streets east of the Bowery was, in Jacob Riis's account, "overrun" by Russian and Polish Jews "to the point of suffocation."

memes.label: Robert Treat Paine
memes.description: Boston philanthropist and housing reformer whose model tenement work was a national example.

memes.label: Robert W. De Forest
memes.description: Counsel to the COS and chair of the Tenement House Committee.

memes.label: Roosevelt Street
memes.description: A short street in the Fourth Ward where Riis photographed one of the last surviving original rear-tenements, capturing the physical form of the earliest tenement type before demolition

memes.label: Russian Jew
memes.description: Russian Jewish immigrant community on Manhattan's Lower East Side during the late nineteenth century.

memes.label: Rutgers Street
memes.description: Lower East Side

memes.label: Saloon
memes.description: The ubiquitous neighborhood drinking establishment of tenement districts — a social institution that Riis viewed ambivalently as both a source of vice and one of the few public gathering spaces available to the poor

memes.label: Sanitary Police
memes.description: The inspection force of the New York City Board of Health responsible for enforcing tenement sanitation regulations in the late 19th century, conducting midnight raids against overcrowding, and policing the markets of the tenement districts.

memes.label: Sanitary Reform
memes.description: The public health and housing reform movement that sought to improve tenement conditions through building regulations, sanitary inspections, and the enforcement of light and ventilation standards

memes.label: Scammel Street
memes.description: Lower East Side

memes.label: School Truancy
memes.description: Chronic absence from school among tenement children, linked by Riis to poverty, child labor, and parental neglect.

memes.label: Scrub
memes.description: The tenement cleaning woman who Riis documented as part of the lowest tier of service labor, often working with stolen pails and brushes to earn subsistence wages.

memes.label: Sebastopol
memes.description: A notorious tenement block in 1890s New York, known by the ironic nickname "Sebastopol" — paired with Hell's Kitchen as a landmark of slum conditions in Jacob Riis's How the Other Half Lives

memes.label: Second Avenue
memes.description: A major north-south avenue on Manhattan's East Side, running parallel to First Avenue through the tenement districts documented by Riis.

memes.label: Seventh Ward
memes.description: A densely populated tenement district on the Lower East Side of Manhattan, heavily Irish in the 1890s, known for some of the worst housing conditions documented by Riis, including Penitentiary Row.

memes.label: Sheriff Street
memes.description: Lower East Side

memes.label: Silas Wood
memes.description: The builder of Gotham Court's rear tenement, whose hatred for his brother John Wood drove him to construct a building deliberately designed to blight the property next door — a monument to revenge that Riis called "a living illustration of the power of hate."

memes.label: Sister Irene
memes.description: Founder of the New York Foundling Asylum, a Catholic charity that cared for abandoned infants in the tenement districts — praised by Riis as a figure of "twenty-one years of loving work" among the city's most vulnerable children.

memes.label: Sixth Ward
memes.description: A notoriously turbulent ward in lower Manhattan, home to the Whyo Gang and a center of crime and vice in the late 19th century.

memes.label: Slum
memes.description: The degraded urban neighborhood ecosystem created by the concentration of tenement housing, poverty, and neglect in 1890s New York, as documented in Jacob Riis's How the Other Half Lives.

memes.label: Small Parks Act (1887)
memes.description: The 1887 New York State legislation that enabled the conversion of slum blocks into public parks — most notably Mulberry Bend Park, which replaced the city's most notorious slum with green space and became Riis's premier example of environmental reform.

memes.label: Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Children
memes.description: A pioneering child welfare organization founded in 1875 that focused on rescuing abused, neglected, and exploited children from New York's tenement districts — the first organization of its kind in the world.

memes.label: Spring Street
memes.description: A street at the northern edge of the Lower East Side tenement belt.

memes.label: Stale-beer Dive
memes.description: The illicit cellar drinking establishments of the Mulberry Bend slum, where the dregs of beer barrels were collected, re-fermented, and sold for a few cents to the poorest of New York's tenement population.

memes.label: Stale-Beer Dive
memes.description: An illicit basement saloon in the slums that sold the dregs of beer kegs for a few cents, described by Riis as the most degraded drinking establishment in the Bend and a primary locus of crime and vice

memes.label: Stanton Street
memes.description: A tenement street on Manhattan's Lower East Side where garment workers and their families lived in the 1890s — the home of a "knee-pants" operator documented by Jacob Riis who worked twelve-hour days for forty-two cents a dozen while supporting four children and a sick wife.

memes.label: State Board of Charities
memes.description: The New York State agency overseeing charitable institutions including almshouses and asylums.

memes.label: State Street
memes.description: Lower Manhattan

memes.label: Street Arab
memes.description: Riis's term for the homeless children of New York — newsboys, bootblacks, and vagrants — who lived on the streets and were the subject of an entire chapter in How the Other Half Lives as both a symbol of tenement failure and a target of charitable rescue

memes.label: Street Arabs
memes.description: Riis's term for the homeless children of New York's streets — newsboys, bootblacks, and vagrants who slept in doorways, boiler rooms, and alleys, forming a distinct underclass of urban childhood in the 1890s.

memes.label: Sullivan Street
memes.description: A street in the Eighth Ward near Thompson Street, part of the African-American settlement area documented by Riis in his chapter on the color line.

memes.label: Swamp Angels
memes.description: An Irish street gang active in the Five Points and Lower East Side tenement districts of the 1890s, named for the swampy terrain of their territory.

memes.label: Sweater
memes.description: A labor middleman who exploited tenement dwellers by subcontracting garment work at starvation wages, epitomizing the intersection of housing and labor exploitation in Riis's New York

memes.label: Sweatshop
memes.description: The exploitative system of garment manufacturing carried out in tenement apartments, where immigrant workers—predominantly Jewish—labored for piece-rate wages under conditions Riis called "the sweating system."

memes.label: Tenement
memes.description: Multi-family rental housing occupied by the urban poor in 19th-century New York, defined by law as a house occupied by three or more families living independently

memes.label: Tenement House Act
memes.description: The series of New York State laws (1867, 1879, 1901) that progressively regulated tenement construction, requiring minimum standards for light, ventilation, and sanitation

memes.label: Tenement-House Act of 1867
memes.description: The first major legislation regulating tenement construction and sanitation in New York City, passed in 1867 as part of a citizens' movement sparked by the threat of cholera and the findings of the Council of Hygiene — described by Jacob Riis as "the first step toward remedial legislation."

memes.label: Tenement House Act of 1901
memes.description: The landmark 'New Law' that replaced the dumbbell design — culmination of the reform movement.

memes.label: Tenement House Commission
memes.description: A government fact-finding body that investigated tenement conditions in New York City during the 1880s, producing mortality statistics and sanitary surveys that provided the evidentiary basis for tenement reform legislation.

memes.label: Tenement House Exhibition
memes.description: The 1900 exhibition organized by Lawrence Veiller that displayed tenement conditions to the public, building support for the 1901 Act.

memes.label: Tenement Reform
memes.description: The movement to improve tenement housing conditions through legislation, inspection, and model building — a cause that Riis both documented and advanced, chronicling the "practical tenement reformers" who fought against landlord opposition and political indifference to transform New York's housing laws

memes.label: The Bend
memes.description: The most notorious slum district in 1890s New York — a warren of rear tenements, stale-beer dives, and mixed immigrant populations centered on Mulberry Street, described by Riis as the symbolic heart of tenement misery

memes.label: The Tenement Child
memes.description: The child growing up in the tenement districts — simultaneously the most vulnerable victim of slum conditions and, in Riis's narrative, the primary justification for reform

memes.label: The Charity Doctor
memes.description: Physicians who served the tenement poor — often working pro bono or for minimal fees — whose testimony about disease, overcrowding, and child mortality provided Riis with clinical evidence for his reform arguments

memes.label: The Immigrant
memes.description: The newly arrived European or Chinese migrant who populated the tenement districts — a composite figure whose experience Riis documents across all twenty-five chapters as both the victim of the housing system and the engine of New York's economic growth

memes.label: The Italian Immigrant
memes.description: The most recent and most marginalized of the major immigrant groups Riis documented — concentrated in the Bend, exploited by the padrone labor system, and subjected to the harshest housing conditions and most intense nativist prejudice

memes.label: The Missionary
memes.description: Religious and charitable workers who lived among the tenement poor, ministering to their spiritual and material needs — figures Riis treats with respect but also with the recognition that individual charity could not substitute for structural reform

memes.label: The Policeman
memes.description: The beat patrolman and sanitary police officer who enforced — or failed to enforce — housing regulations in the tenement districts, positioned by Riis at the frontline of the struggle between reform and corruption

memes.label: The Politician
memes.description: The ward-level political operative who controlled votes, patronage, and police protection in the tenement districts — a figure Riis portrays as both exploiter of and advocate for the immigrant poor

memes.label: The Sweater Worker
memes.description: The tenement dweller — often a married woman or an entire family — who performed piecework garment manufacturing in their own apartment for a sweater middleman, earning starvation wages for twelve-hour days

memes.label: Theodore Roosevelt
memes.description: Police Commissioner of New York City and ally of Jacob Riis.

memes.label: Thompson Street
memes.description: Notorious street on Manhattan's Lower East Side known for moral turpitude and poverty.

memes.label: Tompkins Square
memes.description: A public park in the heart of the 1890s Lower East Side — transformed from a barren sand lot notorious for violent labor riots into a landscaped park that Riis cited as his prime exhibit for the pacifying effect of green space on tenement populations.

memes.label: Tramp / Tramp Army
memes.description: The transient homeless population of post-Civil War New York, described by Riis as an "army" of displaced veterans and itinerant laborers who filled the city's lodging-houses and police stations.

memes.label: Tuberculosis
memes.description: The respiratory disease endemic in dark, unventilated tenement rooms — Riis's most powerful public health argument for reform.

memes.label: Waif / Street Children
memes.description: Abandoned, orphaned, and homeless children of New York's tenement districts, a population that numbered in the thousands and became a primary focus of 19th-century child welfare reform movements.

memes.label: Water Street
memes.description: A waterfront street in 1890s Lower Manhattan, identified by Jacob Riis as "one of the lowest localities in the city" — but also the site of Ellen Collins's pioneering model tenement experiment that proved slum improvement could be profitable.

memes.label: West Side
memes.description: The western tenement districts of Manhattan below the Battery, heavily populated by Irish immigrants in the 1890s and characterized by extreme poverty, stale-beer dives, and some of the worst housing conditions documented in How the Other Half Lives.

memes.label: Whitehall Street
memes.description: Lower Manhattan

memes.label: Whyo Gang
memes.description: A notorious criminal gang that dominated the Sixth Ward and the Bend district of late 19th-century New York until its breakup following the execution of its leader, Driscoll, in the 1880s.

memes.label: Willett Street
memes.description: A narrow Lower East Side street in the old-law tenement district.

memes.label: Working Women's Society
memes.description: A labor advocacy organization in late 1880s New York that investigated and publicized the exploitation of women workers — particularly saleswomen and garment workers — documenting poverty wages, excessive fines, and the structural vulnerability of female laborers.
aliases: Working Women's Society of New York

memes.label: Workingmen's Home
memes.description: The AICP's model tenement on Elizabeth Street (1855), the first purpose-built housing for working families in New York.
